stem: Coming from seaward (IALA region A), what colour and shape is a PORT-hand lateral mark?
choices: Red, can-shaped | Green, conical | Yellow, spherical
explanation: IALA region A: the port mark is red and can-shaped; keep it on your left when entering.
source_ref: Balisage IALA région A — marques latérales

stem: Coming from seaward (region A), what colour and shape is a STARBOARD-hand lateral mark?
choices: Green, conical | Red, can-shaped | Black and red, with spheres
explanation: IALA region A: the starboard mark is green and conical; keep it on your right when entering.
source_ref: Balisage IALA région A — marques latérales

stem: How is a NORTH cardinal mark's topmark recognised, and which side do you pass?
choices: Two cones pointing up; pass to the north | Two cones pointing down; pass to the south | Two cones point-to-point; pass to the west
explanation: North cardinal: two black cones, points UP; safe water is to the north, so pass to the north of the mark.
source_ref: Balisage IALA — marque cardinale Nord

stem: What does a black mark with red horizontal bands, topped by two black spheres, indicate?
choices: An isolated danger surrounded by safe water | The main harbour entrance | A supervised swimming area
explanation: It is an isolated-danger mark: moored ON a small danger surrounded by safe water; white light, group flashing (2).
source_ref: Balisage IALA — marque de danger isolé

stem: How is a SOUTH cardinal mark's topmark recognised, and which side do you pass?
choices: Two cones pointing down; pass to the south | Two cones pointing up; pass to the north | Two cones base to base; pass to the east
explanation: South cardinal: two black cones pointing DOWN; safe water is to the south, so pass to the south of the mark.
source_ref: Balisage IALA — marque cardinale Sud

stem: What does an all-YELLOW mark, possibly with a yellow St Andrew's cross (X) topmark, indicate?
choices: A special area or feature (special mark) | An isolated danger to avoid | The starboard side of the channel
explanation: A special mark: it bounds a special area or feature (bathing zone, channel, pipeline, restricted area) and does not itself mark a navigational danger.
source_ref: Balisage IALA — marque spéciale

stem: What does a mark with red and white vertical stripes and a red spherical topmark indicate?
choices: Safe water all around; pass on either side | An isolated danger to avoid | The starboard side of a channel
explanation: Safe-water mark (mid-channel / landfall): you may pass it on either side.
source_ref: Balisage IALA — marque d'eaux saines

stem: In our waters, when entering a channel from seaward, what is the colour and the shape of the optional topmark on a port-hand mark?
choices: Red, with a red cylinder (can) topmark | Green, with a green cone topmark point up | Red, with a red cone topmark point up
explanation: The port-hand mark is red and can-shaped, and its optional topmark is a single red cylinder (can). You leave it to port (on your left) when entering.
source_ref: IALA R1001 §2.1.3 — marque latérale bâbord (région A)

stem: In our waters, when entering a channel from seaward, which light and optional topmark identify a starboard-hand mark?
choices: Green light, with a green cone topmark point up | Red light, with a red cylinder (can) topmark | Green light, with a green cone topmark point down
explanation: The starboard-hand mark is green and cone-shaped; it shows a green light and its optional topmark is a single green cone point up. You leave it to starboard (on your right) when entering.
source_ref: IALA R1001 §2.1.3 — marque latérale tribord (région A)

stem: Where a channel divides, you see a red mark with a single broad green horizontal band. What does it tell you?
choices: The preferred channel is to starboard | The preferred channel is to port | The channel is blocked and you must turn back
explanation: A red mark with a broad green horizontal band (red can topmark) shows that the preferred channel is to starboard. Such preferred-channel marks display a composite group-flashing (2+1) light.
source_ref: IALA R1001 §2.1.5 — marques de chenal préféré (région A)

stem: Which topmark and light rhythm identify an East cardinal mark, which must be passed on its east side?
choices: Two black cones base to base; white VQ(3) every 5 s or Q(3) every 10 s | Two black cones point to point; white VQ(9) every 10 s | Two black cones base to base; white group-occulting light
explanation: The East cardinal has two black cones base to base, a black body with a broad yellow horizontal band, and a white VQ(3) every 5 s or Q(3) every 10 s light. You pass to the east of the mark.
source_ref: IALA R1001 §2.2.4 — marque cardinale Est

stem: A cardinal mark shows two black cones one above the other, both pointing up, with a body black above and yellow below. On which side should you pass?
choices: To the north of the mark | To the south of the mark | To the east of the mark
explanation: This topmark (two black cones point up, body black over yellow, white very-quick or quick flashing light) marks a North cardinal: the safe water is to the north, so you pass to the north of the mark.
source_ref: IALA R1001 §2.2.4 — marque cardinale Nord

stem: A mark shows two black cones arranged point to point, a yellow body with a single broad black horizontal band, and a white light flashing in groups of nine. On which side must you pass?
choices: To the west of the mark | To the east of the mark | To the north of the mark
explanation: The two black cones point to point, the yellow body with a broad black horizontal band and the white VQ(9) every 10 s (or Q(9) every 15 s) light identify the West cardinal mark: you pass to the west of it.
source_ref: IALA R1001 §2.2.4 — marque cardinale Ouest

stem: A mark carries two black cones with points downward, a yellow body on top and black below, and shows a white light of quick flashes Q(6) followed by one long flash. Which mark is it?
choices: A South cardinal mark | A North cardinal mark | A West cardinal mark
explanation: The two cones pointing downward, the body yellow above and black below, and the white light VQ(6)+one long flash every 10 s (or Q(6)+one long flash every 15 s) identify the South cardinal mark: you pass to the south of it.
source_ref: IALA R1001 §2.2.4 — marque cardinale Sud

stem: A mark has a black body with one or more broad red horizontal bands, a topmark of two black spheres one above the other, and a white light flashing in groups of two. What does it indicate?
choices: An isolated danger surrounded by safe water, on which it is moored | Safe water all around, which may be passed on either side | A particular regulated zone, not a danger in itself
explanation: The black body with broad red horizontal bands, the topmark of two black spheres and the white group-flashing (2) light identify the isolated danger mark, moored on (or above) an isolated danger surrounded by safe water.
source_ref: IALA R1001 §2.3 — marque de danger isolé

stem: A mark shows red and white vertical stripes, a topmark of a single red sphere, and a white isophase light (or occulting, one long flash every 10 s, or Morse "A"). What may you do when passing it?
choices: Pass it on either side: safe water all around | Pass only to the north: isolated danger all around | Avoid it on one side only: it is moored on a danger
explanation: The red and white vertical stripes, the single red sphere topmark and the white isophase / occulting / one long flash every 10 s / Morse "A" light identify a safe-water mark (mid-channel, landfall): you may pass it on either side.
source_ref: IALA R1001 §2.4 — marque d'eaux saines

stem: A mark is entirely yellow, may carry a yellow St Andrew's cross ("X") topmark, and shows a yellow light. What does it delimit?
choices: A particular zone or feature (ODAS, regulated area, pipeline, anchorage…) | An isolated danger surrounded by safe water | The starboard side of the channel to follow
explanation: An all-yellow mark, with an optional yellow St Andrew's cross topmark and a yellow light (rhythm different from those reserved for cardinals, isolated danger and safe water) is a special mark: it delimits a particular zone or feature (ODAS, regulated area, pipeline, anchorage area…), not a danger in itself.
source_ref: IALA R1001 §2.5 — marque spéciale

stem: At sea, what does the regulation say about discharging rubbish and hydrocarbons?
choices: It is prohibited | It is free beyond 3 miles | It is allowed at night
explanation: Discharging rubbish and hydrocarbons into the sea is prohibited; waste must be brought back ashore.
source_ref: Code de l'environnement, art. L.218-11 et s. / MARPOL annexes I et V

stem: What should the boater do with waste produced aboard?
choices: Bring it ashore to harbour facilities | Throw it overboard offshore | Burn it aboard
explanation: Keep it aboard and dispose of it ashore in the harbour's collection facilities.
source_ref: MARPOL annexe V / Code de l'environnement — déchets rapportés à terre

stem: In the Mediterranean, anchoring on Posidonia seagrass beds is:
choices: Regulated, even prohibited (protected beds) | Free and harmless | Recommended because the anchor holds well there
explanation: Regulated, and prohibited in many areas: the anchor tears up these protected beds, vital to marine life; use the designated anchoring zones or buoys.
source_ref: PREMAR Méditerranée, arrêté n° 123/2019, art. 6 — mouillage / posidonie

stem: At night, an underway power-driven vessel under 50 m shows sidelights. What colour is the PORT sidelight?
choices: Red | Green | White
explanation: COLREG: port sidelight RED, starboard green; plus a white masthead light and a white sternlight.
source_ref: RIPAM règle 23 — Feux d'un navire à moteur

stem: In sight of another vessel, a power-driven vessel sounds ONE short blast. What is it signalling?
choices: “I am altering to starboard” | “I am altering to port” | “I am operating astern”
explanation: COLREG rule 34: one short blast = “I am altering my course to starboard”.
source_ref: RIPAM règle 34 — Signaux de manœuvre (1 son bref)

stem: What do THREE short blasts from a power-driven vessel in sight of another mean?
choices: “I am operating astern propulsion” | “I am altering to port” | “I am aground”
explanation: COLREG rule 34: three short blasts = “I am operating astern propulsion”.
source_ref: RIPAM règle 34 — Signaux de manœuvre (3 sons brefs)

stem: What do at least FIVE short and rapid blasts mean?
choices: Doubt / a warning about the other's manoeuvre | An invitation to cross ahead | A distress call
explanation: COLREG rule 34(d): at least five short rapid blasts signal doubt about the other vessel's intentions or manoeuvre (a warning).
source_ref: RIPAM règle 34 — Signal de doute

stem: In restricted visibility (fog), what signal does a power-driven vessel underway and making way sound?
choices: One prolonged blast at least every 2 minutes | Three short blasts every minute | No signal is required
explanation: COLREG rule 35: one prolonged blast at intervals of no more than 2 minutes.
source_ref: RIPAM règle 35 — Signaux par visibilité réduite

stem: Which of these is a recognised PYROTECHNIC distress signal at sea?
choices: A red parachute flare | A flashing green light | A hoisted yellow flag
explanation: A red parachute rocket flare is a recognised distress signal; orange smoke also serves by day.
source_ref: RIPAM annexe IV §1(i) — signaux de détresse (fusée à parachute rouge)

stem: At night, a vessel under 50 m at anchor shows:
choices: An all-round white light | An all-round red light | Two green lights in a vertical line
explanation: COLREG rule 30: an all-round white light forward; by day, a black ball forward.
source_ref: RIPAM règle 30 — Navire au mouillage

stem: The Beaufort scale, measuring wind force, is graduated from 0 to what value?
explanation: The Beaufort scale runs from force 0 (calm) to force 12 (hurricane).
source_ref: Échelle de Beaufort 0–12 (OMM / Météo-France)

stem: On a nautical chart, relative to what level are the depths (soundings) given?
choices: Chart datum (lowest tides) | Mean sea level | High-water level
explanation: Soundings are referred to chart datum, close to the level of the lowest tides: actual depth is usually greater.
source_ref: SHOM — zéro hydrographique (sondes des cartes marines)

stem: What is the “tidal range” (marnage)?
choices: The height difference between high and low water | The speed of the tidal stream | The duration of a tide
explanation: The tidal range is the height difference between a high water and the following (or preceding) low water.
source_ref: SHOM — marnage (pleine mer / basse mer consécutives)

stem: Between what values does the tidal coefficient vary?
choices: Between 20 and 120 | Between 0 and 100 | Between 1 and 12
explanation: The tidal coefficient ranges from 20 (very weak neap) to 120 (very strong spring); 95 marks the threshold of large tides.
source_ref: SHOM — coefficient de marée (échelle 20 à 120)

stem: What is the “rule of twelfths” used for?
choices: To estimate the water height at a given hour of the tide | To calculate the wind force | To measure drift from the current
explanation: It estimates the height of water at a given time between low and high water (1/12, 2/12, 3/12, 3/12, 2/12, 1/12 per hour).
source_ref: Marées — règle des douzièmes (méthode standard, SHOM)

stem: What is the tidal “slack” (étale)?
choices: When the water level stops changing (slack of the level) | When the current is strongest | The difference between high and low water
explanation: The brief moment, at high or low water, when the level stops changing (slack of the level). The current's turn (slack of stream) is a related but distinct event that need not coincide.
source_ref: SHOM — étale (de niveau)

stem: According to the rule of twelfths, by which fraction of the tidal range does the water rise during each successive hour, from low water to high water?
explanation: Under the rule of twelfths, the tidal range changes hour by hour by 1/12, 2/12, 3/12, 3/12, 2/12 then 1/12.
source_ref: Règle des douzièmes (méthode de l'annuaire des marées)

stem: Between which values do the recognised tidal coefficients range, from extreme neap to extreme spring?
choices: From 20 to 120 | From 0 to 100 | From 45 to 95
explanation: The tidal coefficient characterises the amplitude of the tide. Recognised values run from 20 (extreme neap) to 120 (extreme spring); 45 is the average neap and 95 the average spring.
source_ref: SHOM — coefficient de marée

stem: What is the name for the falling tide, during which the level drops towards low water?
choices: The ebb (jusant) | The flood (flot) | Slack water
explanation: The flood (flot) is the rising tide, during which the level rises towards high water; the ebb (jusant) is the falling tide, during which the level drops towards low water.
source_ref: SHOM — flot et jusant

stem: A coastal area has a tidal range greater than 4 m. How is this tidal regime described?
choices: Macrotidal | Mesotidal | Microtidal
explanation: Tidal range is the height difference between a high water and the following low water. A range below 2 m is microtidal, 2 to 4 m is mesotidal, and above 4 m is macrotidal.
source_ref: SHOM — marnage

stem: On the Channel and Atlantic coasts the tide is of the semi-diurnal type. How many high waters and low waters occur in one day?
choices: Two high waters and two low waters | One high water and one low water | Three high waters and three low waters
explanation: A semi-diurnal tide produces two high waters and two low waters per day, the tidal cycle lasting about 12 h 25.
source_ref: SHOM — marée semi-diurne et cycle

stem: Which tidal coefficient corresponds to the mean spring high and low waters (PMVE/BMVE)?
choices: Coefficient 95 | Coefficient 120 | Coefficient 45
explanation: The mean spring high and low waters (PMVE/BMVE) correspond to coefficient 95, whereas the highest and lowest astronomical tides correspond to coefficient 120.
source_ref: SHOM — niveaux caractéristiques de la marée

stem: When the Earth, Moon and Sun are aligned (full or new moon), which type of tide occurs?
choices: Spring tides (large range) | Neap tides (small range) | No tide (permanent slack water)
explanation: At syzygy — full or new moon, when Earth, Moon and Sun are aligned — the tidal waves add together: these are spring tides, with a large range. At quadrature (first or last quarter) the waves oppose each other, producing neap tides with a small range.
source_ref: SHOM — vives-eaux et mortes-eaux

stem: In France, to which reference level is the chart datum — to which the soundings on nautical charts are referred — set?
choices: Near the lowest astronomical tides | At mean sea level | Near the highest astronomical tides
explanation: Chart datum is the common reference level for nautical charts and tide tables. In France it is set near the lowest astronomical tides (LAT/PBMA), a level the sea only very exceptionally falls below; the actual depth is therefore generally greater than the charted sounding.
source_ref: SHOM — zéro hydrographique (ZH)

stem: What is meant by slack water of level (étale de niveau)?
choices: The brief moment, at high or low water, when the water level no longer changes | The moment when the tidal current reverses, always coinciding with high or low water | The moment when the tidal current speed is at its maximum between two tides
explanation: Slack water of level is the brief moment, at high or low water, when the water level no longer changes; it is distinct from the turn of the tidal current (slack of current), which does not necessarily coincide with it.
source_ref: SHOM — étale

stem: Above what engine power is the permis plaisance required?
choices: More than 4.5 kW (≈ 6 hp) | More than 1 kW | More than 50 hp
explanation: The licence is required to drive a motor pleasure boat whose power exceeds 4.5 kW (about 6 hp).
source_ref: Décret n° 2007-1167 du 2 août 2007, art. 2 — permis exigé au-delà de 4,5 kW

stem: From what age can the permis plaisance (côtière option) be obtained?
choices: 16 years | 14 years | 18 years
explanation: The minimum age to obtain the permis plaisance is 16.
source_ref: Décret n° 2007-1167 du 2 août 2007, art. 3 — âge minimal (16 ans)

stem: Which VHF channel is reserved for distress, urgency and safety watch, and for calling?
choices: Channel 16 | Channel 6 | Channel 72
explanation: Channel 16 is the international distress, urgency, safety and calling channel.
source_ref: UIT, Règlement des radiocommunications, appendice 18 — VHF canal 16 (156,8 MHz)

stem: A radio message beginning with “MAYDAY” corresponds to which priority level?
choices: Distress | Urgency | Safety
explanation: “MAYDAY” = distress: grave and imminent danger, immediate help. “PAN PAN” = urgency; “SÉCURITÉ” = safety.
source_ref: UIT, Règlement des radiocommunications, art. 32 — détresse (MAYDAY)

stem: A radio message beginning with “PAN PAN” corresponds to which priority level?
choices: Urgency | Distress | Safety (weather bulletin)
explanation: “PAN PAN” = urgency: the safety of a vessel or person is at risk, without the grave and imminent danger that warrants distress (MAYDAY).
source_ref: UIT, Règlement des radiocommunications, art. 33 — urgence (PAN PAN)

stem: Within the 300-metre coastal band from the shore, what is the navigation speed generally limited to?
choices: 5 knots | 20 knots | No speed limit
explanation: Unless a local rule says otherwise, speed is limited to 5 knots within the 300-metre band, for the safety of swimmers.
source_ref: Arrêté du préfet maritime (bande des 300 m, 5 nœuds) ; CGCT art. L.2213-23

stem: In a narrow channel, to which side must a vessel generally keep?
choices: The channel edge on its starboard side | The middle of the channel | The edge on its port side
explanation: COLREG rule 9: keep as near as is safe to the outer limit of the channel that lies on its STARBOARD side.
source_ref: RIPAM règle 9 — Chenaux étroits

stem: Two power-driven vessels meet on directly opposite (head-on) courses with risk of collision. What must each do?
choices: Each alters to starboard | Each alters to port | The faster one keeps clear, the other holds course
explanation: COLREG rule 14: each alters course to STARBOARD so they pass port to port.
source_ref: RIPAM règle 14 — Navires faisant des routes opposées

stem: Two power-driven vessels are on crossing courses with risk of collision. Which one must keep out of the way?
choices: The one that has the other on its starboard side | The one that has the other on its port side | The larger of the two vessels
explanation: COLREG rule 15: the vessel that has the other on its OWN starboard side gives way; the other (stand-on) keeps course and speed.
source_ref: RIPAM règle 15 — Routes qui se croisent

stem: One vessel is overtaking another. Which must keep out of the way?
choices: The overtaking vessel | The vessel being overtaken | Always the sailing vessel
explanation: COLREG rule 13: the OVERTAKING vessel keeps clear of the one being overtaken, whatever the type of either vessel.
source_ref: RIPAM règle 13 — Navire qui en rattrape un autre

stem: Not overtaking and outside a narrow channel, a power-driven vessel meets a vessel under sail. Which one keeps clear?
choices: The power-driven vessel | The sailing vessel | Neither, both hold their course
explanation: COLREG rule 18: the power-driven vessel keeps out of the way of the sailing vessel.
source_ref: RIPAM règle 18 — Responsabilités réciproques

stem: You are the stand-on vessel that must keep course and speed, but the other vessel takes no action and collision becomes imminent. What may you do?
choices: Manoeuvre to avoid the collision yourself | Hold course and speed no matter what | Speed up to cross ahead
explanation: COLREG rule 17: if the give-way vessel fails to act, the stand-on vessel may, then must, manoeuvre to avoid collision on its own.
source_ref: RIPAM règle 17 — Navire privilégié

stem: Up to what distance from a shelter does the côtière option of the permis plaisance authorise navigation?
choices: 6 nautical miles | 2 nautical miles | With no distance limit
explanation: The côtière option allows navigation up to 6 nautical miles from a shelter, day and night, regardless of engine power.
source_ref: Décret n° 2007-1167 du 2 août 2007, art. 2 — portée de l'option côtière (6 milles)

stem: Which navigation programme does design category “C” of a pleasure boat correspond to?
choices: Inshore (force 6, 2 m waves) | Unrestricted ocean | Sheltered waters only
explanation: Category C = “inshore”: designed for winds up to force 6 and waves up to 2 m. (A = ocean, B = offshore, D = sheltered waters.)
source_ref: Directive 2013/53/UE, annexe I, partie A — catégories de conception (C : force 6, vagues 2 m)

stem: For coastal navigation (up to 6 miles from a shelter), what minimum buoyancy must the personal flotation device have under Division 240?
explanation: Division 240: at least 100 newtons for the coastal zone (beyond 2 and up to 6 miles); 50 N inshore, 150 N offshore.
source_ref: Division 240, art. 240-2.04 §3 — EIF niveau 100 (zone côtière 2–6 MN)

stem: How many people may legally be carried aboard a pleasure boat?
choices: The maximum set by the builder | As many as there are lifejackets aboard | No limit at sea
explanation: At most the number set by the builder, shown on the boat's builder's plate / design category.
source_ref: Division 240, art. 240-2.01 §1 — nombre maximal de personnes (plaque constructeur)

stem: On a tiller-steered motorboat, why must the driver wear the engine kill-cord attached to themselves?
choices: It stops the engine if the driver falls overboard | It increases engine speed | It serves as an anti-theft device in port
explanation: If they fall overboard, the cord pulls free and instantly stops the engine: the boat cannot run off or injure the person in the water.
source_ref: Division 240, art. 240-2.01 §7 — coupe-circuit (barre franche / déporté / VNM)
